replies.author: @goldengibson1958
replies.text:
⁠@Christobanistanianbeen a lifelong fan of the Alien universe and I have watched every entry (including the ill fated AVP saga). I have watched all 4 episodes of Alien Earth so far, and the plot holes are so big a colonial marine could make the gap. 

The core premise of making children into immortals so that Wonder Boy could finally have an intelligent conversation is just lazy. The top secret deep space Weiland-Yutani spacecraft being able to crash land in the middle of a populated city owned by the worlds youngest trillionaire without any interventions prior to its crash is also extremely lazy and defies belief. 

The fact that it was intentionally sabotaged causing the crash, by the cyborg whose whole mission is exclusive to the preservation and containment of the specimens, defies belief. If containment was the goal (which he clearly states, and the owner of Weiland-Yutani is pissed that her specimens are subject to capture by Prodigy), then letting the specimens loose on the ship and crashing it was beyond moronic and incompetent.

The “children” being used as soldiers without an authority directing their actions is stupefyingly unbelievable. The “children” being able to communicate with agents of Weiland-Yutani without being monitored is stupefyingly unbelievable. 

The “children” being able to move about the secure research facility resulting in access to Pridigy’s #1 asset acquisition (the xenomorphs), is stupefyingly unbelievable.

The aberrant behavior of the redheaded immortal going unreported, and her not being contained to determine cause of her violence against her authorities is also stupefyingly unbelievable. 

The writing in this series so far feels like it’s patched together from quick ideas left on sticky notes scattered in no particular order all over the office. It doesn’t hold a candle to its predecessors. 

Sure the sets and cinematography is decent for a TV series, but honestly it’s really frustrating the way they destroy any hint of suspense by blasting these 6 frame rapid fire clips in the way of exposition. 

I would much rather have had Ridley Scott finish what he started with Prometheus and Covenant, or adapt the excellent Aliens: Labyrinth, or Aliens: Music of the spears. 

This is Disneyfied slop writing/storytelling damaging yet another beloved and well fleshed out creative world of content. They just can’t seem to help themselves. The problem here is that the tropes are the same no matter what IP they are working with.

replies.author: @Christobanistanian
replies.text:
@goldengibson1958 I see, you're "one of those."  a "true" fan who can no longer see the forest for the trees, and by that I mean  you take things way too seriously.

None of these things are "lazy," you just choose to interpret them that way.  

For example, the Prodigy guy didn't actually do everything his whole life "just" to have an interesting conversation.  That whole rant was instead intended to show you his megalomania, not to honestly and fully explain his life's motivations.

As for the rest, I won't go over each and every item, but you seem to be assuming nothing else could possibly be explained or become clearer as the series progresses.  For you, no other possible explanations but the writers are idiots, never that you are impatient or maybe you didn't quite understand what you were seeing, like with the rant.

This sort of tunnel vision is not unexpected from someone who has read every book, obsessed over every piece of Aliens film, and picked over every detail.  You people don't seem to understand that most people don't give a shit about minor inconsistencies, nor do we expect everyone in the universe to behave precisely the same, as you would personally expect.

replies.author: @GideonRavenor712
replies.text:
@stateazure Honestly?

Debatable for the Alien Universe, from what I know.

In Alien 1 Ash is able to get a response via Mother from Weyland-Yutani after he sends a report back about the situation on the Nostromo, and although the time-scale of Alien is tough to rationalize as to how long it spans, it wasn't an instantaneous thing but in space terms it was fairly quick.

Aliens (2) IIRC it took . . . weeks to a couple months? For the distress call from Hadley's Hope to both reach authorities and start to mobilize a response via the Colonial Marines. Newt had been living alone for a little while by the time they arrived.

Alien 3, again I can't remember the time scale, but despite how remote and infrequent any visitors to the planet they're on are, Weyland-Yutani got there pretty quick at the end.

Prometheus and Alien Covenant they never even attempt to send any messages back to Earth, aside from IIRC David's status update at the end of Covenant. However, technologically these take place earliest in the timeline.

Personally? My bigger issue with the sequence around the Maginot 4 is why didn't Weyland-Yutani immediately try to intercept it in orbit of Earth? They know what is aboard, and they got their almost ideal situation they'll spend the next 4 movies chronologically chasing for. All they had to do was stop the ship or have their Android ensure it lands somewhere they own.

@thedragonlee76 100 mln made in China only gets you 20 mln in returns. The Chinese box office looks good for PR purposes, but the PRC taking an 80% cut of the box office makes it kinda irrelevant market to chase after. Even European markets only return 45% of the box office to Hollywood. The US box office will remain the biggest and most lucrative market, with a 55% return of the box office. What doomed Hollywood was the replacement of the old guard of studio heads, who may not have understood movies, but they did understand the creatives and recognized when something good was pitched to them, if only because they trusted the creatives. 

Nowadays studioheads are creative voids coming from outside of the industry. Soulless corporate managers, who still don't understand movies, but understand actual creatives even less, and are thus unwilling to take a chance on interesting pitches. That and the rise of movie budgets has made studio heads reluctant to take risks, so they prefer to make certain bets. Sequels, prequels and every 'quel in between. IP's that they think that people know and will bring in an audience. Add to that the rise of the untalented DIE hire slash activist and you have a recipe for movie disasters.

I thought the same thing. Entrusting this to children makes no sense. Normal children are very sensitive and easily traumatized.
Children that age would see mutilated corpses, while even veterans have a hard time dealing with it.
I've worked with former military personnel who told me that the hardest position was sniper because they saw heads exploding through their scopes. It was extremely gory and traumatic. So it would be enough to drive kids crazy.
They wouldn't even remember they were in robot bodies and would start crying and calling for their moms nonstop.
It's as if the script's authors were clones who grew up in a test tube and had no family or childhood.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text: The lost boys are noise. If they were accomplished superhuman fighters, then it would mean something if the alien cut through them like ribbons. But giving them vague power means you can dial up or down their power level. And they have Wendy a sword, so I guess she's hyper powered with a super computer in her brain AND she's a ninja, I guess. If Ripley can hold her own against multiple aliens, then creating superhumans would break the dynamic. Even hundreds of years later (Alien 4), humans are impressed enough by the Alien, that their version of a supersoldier is Ripley with Alien DNA. If robots exceed the power of the aliens, then.... why would they research an inferior weapon? They're already established a power level for robots. A hundred years later, the character played by Winona Ryder was presumably many generations of android later and she couldn't do ANYTHING about the alien.

replies.author: @Simon-xc5oy
replies.text: @JurassicRod Yes I can understand that point of view. But it was incredibly depressing. You went from the rip roaring triumph of Aliens, with Ripley getting an adopted daughter and Newt a replacement Mom. And Ripley got over her fear of androids thanks to Bishop and had a love interest / equal in Hicks. Then a few years later it was - Kill everyone you care about, infect Ripley and kill her. The end. Alien 3 was the first Alien film I was old enough to see in the Cinema. And I went with three of my mates, we knew it could not top Aliens but we hoped and expected something almost as good. And in five or ten minutes in the cinema, we could hear people moaning about what they were doing. At the end we all walked out subdued and depressed as hell. The worst thing is, a lot of the other other scripts, ideas, leaks etc that were going to be used and came to light over the years were all better than Alien 3. The wooden planet monks version was fascinating and is a leaked script. Ripley survives and is not infected and Newt and Hicks etc remain in hypersleep. The another version where Newt, Hicks and Bishop are the leads if Weaver did not want to appear again is also pretty good, she remains in hypersleep in that one. Its just too much to take in killing off all the characters in one film.

Part way through the filming Scott went on some bender about the Gods and how life was created on Earth and decided to change it all to not being a prequel to Alien, but a search for Gods and mans origins. When you add in Lindelof as a writer too its no wonder its a confusing mess of ideas and plot threads that get started and then dropped or altered. The black goo does whatever they need it to do per scene. The idiots get lost in the tunnels despite having a set of mapping drones showing them how they got in there and where they have been and so on. And the Engineers are ruined. Big Bald Blokes in a suit are just not what the original one was in Alien. Scott wrecked it. That creature should have been more Alien than the Alien and if they were the creators of the creature it should have been so Alien it was incomprehensible. And not an Android on an ego trip. That is the worst change of all. It reminds me of what they did with Doctor Who over the Chibnall and RTD recent runs, changing the character from what he was for decades to something so different it may as well be called something else. And in both cases it ruined the I.P's totally.

replies.author: @uvicjames
replies.text:
Yes, the alien is portrayed as a stealthy hunter in some scenes, but in other scenes it just rips everyone apart with no interest in hiding. It's not consistent at all. Aren't they supposed to be abducting some humans to serve as hosts anyhow? It would be dragging people closer to the eggs.

Also, zero chance any creature is going to be able to slaughter a room full of people with their fingers on the trigger of those pulse rifles. Every single security force member just freezes and does nothing.  As you mention, they would be conditioned and upgraded with implants.

replies.author: @loupkobalt
replies.text:
@uvicjames Yes, that's exactly that. The xenomorph is scary because it captures its prey, not because it kills them.
Let's say it doesn't have eggs, in which case it will simply hunt for food. When they are alone, they flee large gatherings of humans and seek out isolated humans.
This xenomorph behaves as if it were in a pack, even though it is all alone.

Then there are eggs in the cargo. Wouldn't the xenomorph's instinct drive it to put the eggs in a safe place? Ants already do this. Ants are less intelligent and fight from hive to hive.
On the other hand, the xenomorph in Alien 3 refuses to touch Ripley even though he is not the son of the queen larva she has inside her. They live in hives but are more intelligent than ants. They all help each other even if they don't come from the same queen. There's a good chance the xenomorph will take care of the eggs to restart a colony even if they aren't its brothers. And it's even possible that they came from the same nest.

Doesn't it have the ability to turn prey into eggs when it's deprived of an egg, as the deleted scenes from the first film suggest?

And even if these implants are so common, given that the guy partying also has them, I think, the authorities must have tools that would allow them to control an out-of-control cyborg.
The police conduct a check and come across an aggressive cyborg. They take out a remote control and deactivate all his implants, for example.
The army must have these kinds of devices.

It's clear they're not fans. They don't know the license and couldn't care less.

replies.author: @Mereologist
replies.text:
It makes sense according to the rules they've established:  only children are flexible enough and the bodies themselves can't change so it would be weirder in the long run to have a sixty-year-old in a twelve-year-old body.  But it does beg other questions which they didn't ask at all.  Like why make them look human at all?  Or - to be specific - why give a robot breasts?

There's literally no purpose this can serve because the robot cannot reproduce in that way.  In fact, it is only useful for DECEIVING other humans and causing problems.  Androids can be programmed to not take advantage, but humans can't (and why do future androids have such greatly reduced strength?).  If the point is to give other humans cues to react properly, making your cyborg look like a waifish girl is an overt fail.

This now also poisons the other movies.  Why not make the OTHER robots purple or something so everyone knows they're robots?  On a small ship this isn't necessary but not anywhere else.  If they're all identical, why don't they have tags so people know which one is theirs?  We do this for cars and other property.  They're undermining all the other movies and making them worse too...

author: @Surgicaldamage
text:
The only genuinely good thing about "Alien Earth ", are the props and quality of set pieces. They get a 10/10 from me. 

But that also contributes to my disappointment, because it shows that the studio had no excuse to fail.

The "Alien", is not an action set piece, It's a horror that should never escaped "LV4 26" beyond the vacuum of space, a cold misfortune to which, there are no answers or reasons, no history and no future. 

On film, there's only one way to restore that level of impact.

Put it back into the shadows, and rebuild its name, with suspense, uncertainty, discomfort that something is around, and people (who we don't want to die Disney!!) are missing. 

This stupid show, has tacked the alien, into a poorly written, "Mary Sue", power ranger  Peter Pan butter biscuit cookie dunking allegory soap sucking opera, about synthetic artificial Ghost in the shell, ripoffs.

And "Alien Earth", couldn't even draw me in for 2 whole hours to do that.

And to finish with rock music? Like I'm supposed to think" what I just witnessed was so new and cool 😎"?

The fact that it has other creatures in it, because the developers can't build fear, suspense and character, into anything on screen?

In a nutshell, this was expensive props, wasted on rubbish assembly, vomit writing, sewage pacing and editing.

If Disney really insists on rebooting the "Mary Sue synthetic edition" and her band of power puff boys?

Can't they,( for my sake, 🙏) make it digestibility coherent next time.
